title: Prove merkleProofLength is bounded and non-zero across all circuits
description: BinaryMerkleRoot accepts merkleProofLength as a private input with no range check enforcing 1 <= depth <= MAX_DEPTH. A prover can supply depth=0 (making the leaf itself the root, bypassing tree membership) or depth > MAX_DEPTH (reading uninitialized sibling slots). Add Num2Bits + LessThan constraints bounding depth in HumanUniqueness, AgentPolicy, Delegation, and ModelInstanceBinding. Write a formal property file (Certora-style invariant spec) asserting that for any satisfying witness, 1 <= merkleProofLength <= MAX_DEPTH.

title: Domain-separate nullifier hashes across circuit types
description: HumanUniqueness computes nullifier = Poseidon2(scope, secret), AgentPolicy computes nullifier = Poseidon2(credentialCommitment, sessionNonce), and Delegation has its own nullifier. All three use Poseidon2 with no domain tag, creating a theoretical collision risk if a field element happens to serve as both a scope and a credentialCommitment. Prepend a unique domain separator constant (e.g., Poseidon3(DOMAIN_HUMAN, scope, secret)) to each circuit's nullifier. Formally prove that the nullifier domains are disjoint under the Poseidon collision-resistance assumption.

title: Verify expiry check uses strict inequality and handles boundary
description: AgentPolicy checks currentTimestamp < expiryTimestamp via LessThan(64). The Solidity side checks staleness via a 5-minute window around block.timestamp. Formally verify that (1) the circuit's LessThan is strict (not <=), (2) there is no off-by-one where expiryTimestamp == currentTimestamp passes, and (3) the 64-bit range checks on both values prevent a wraparound where a huge expiryTimestamp overflows LessThan. Produce a counterexample witness file if any property fails, and a passing property spec if they hold.

title: Prove cumulative bit encoding is exhaustively enforced
description: The Delegation circuit enforces bit4 => bits 2+3 and bit3 => bit2 on the delegatee scope. Verify this is also enforced in AgentPolicy (it should be, since an agent minting a credential with bit4=1, bit3=0 would violate the invariant). Write an exhaustive property: for every satisfying assignment, the 64-bit decomposition of permissionBitmask satisfies all implication rules. Additionally verify that the subset check (delegateeScope & ~delegatorScope == 0) composes correctly with cumulative encoding — i.e., if delegatorScope is valid and delegateeScope is a subset, then delegateeScope is also valid.

title: Prove scope commitment chain is unforgeable across delegation hops
description: The delegation chain links hops via scopeCommitment = Poseidon(scope, credCommitment). Formally verify the binding property: given a valid delegation proof where previousScopeCommitment matches the prior hop's output, it is infeasible to produce a witness where the delegatee's actual scope exceeds the delegator's scope. This requires proving that Poseidon is used as a commitment scheme (binding under collision resistance) and that the subset check in the circuit is correctly wired to the same signals that feed the commitment. Produce a Halo2-style MockProver test or a Circom witness-gen adversarial harness that attempts to break the chain link.

title: Cross-chain root sync via EIP-1186 storage proofs
description: Deploy IdentityRegistry on Arbitrum and Polygon alongside Base Sepolia, then implement a CrossChainRootRelay contract that accepts EIP-1186 storage proofs to sync humanTree and agentTree roots across chains without a trusted relayer. The relay verifies the source chain's state root (available via L1 block hash precompiles on L2s) and extracts the registry's root slot value. This lets a proof generated against Base's tree verify on Arbitrum or Polygon with ~30-root-buffer latency.

title: Bind sessionNonce to chainId to prevent cross-chain replay
description: The current nullifierHash and sessionNonce have no chain binding — a valid handshake proof verified on Base can be replayed on Arbitrum if both share the same Merkle root (which cross-chain sync enables). Fix by changing the nullifier derivation in all three circuits to include a chainId public input: nullifier = Poseidon3(scope, secret, chainId) for HumanUniqueness, and similarly for AgentPolicy and Delegation. The on-chain verifier must assert chainId == block.chainid. Without this, deploying to a second chain creates an immediate replay vector.

title: Add 30-root history buffer to humanTree (parity with agentTree)
description: IdentityRegistry maintains a 30-root history buffer for agentTree but humanTree has no equivalent — any new enrollment immediately invalidates in-flight human proofs. This is already painful on a single chain but becomes a hard blocker for cross-chain: storage-proof relay adds ~10 minutes of latency, during which any enrollment on the source chain makes all relayed roots stale. Add the same ring-buffer pattern (uint256[30] humanRootHistory + isKnownHumanRoot()) used by agentTree. This is a prerequisite for the cross-chain root relay.

title: Multi-chain provider abstraction in @bolyra/sdk
description: The SDK currently hardcodes a single ethers provider for Base Sepolia. Add a ChainRegistry that maps chainId → {registryAddress, verifierAddresses, rpcUrl} for Base, Arbitrum, and Polygon. verifyHandshake() should accept an optional chainId parameter and route to the correct on-chain registry. Include a resolveNearestRoot(chainId) helper that checks root staleness across chains and picks the freshest verified root, abstracting cross-chain latency from the developer.

title: Off-chain verification with batched on-chain root anchoring
description: For high-throughput multi-chain scenarios, verifying every handshake on-chain is cost-prohibitive even on L2s. Implement a BatchVerifier contract that accepts a Merkle root of N verified session hashes and anchors it on-chain in a single tx. The SDK generates and verifies proofs off-chain (snarkjs/rapidsnark), accumulates session hashes into a local Merkle tree, and submits the batch root periodically. Include a verifySessionInclusion(sessionHash, merkleProof, batchRoot) view function for after-the-fact audit. Target: 100x gas reduction for apps doing >10 handshakes/minute.

title: Define application/bolyra-proof+cbor content type and envelope format
description: The protocol lacks a standardized wire format for proof exchange. Define an IANA-registrable media type (application/bolyra-proof+cbor) with a CDDL schema specifying the envelope structure: proof bytes, public signals array, circuit identifier, proving system tag (groth16/plonk), and protocol version. This enables interop between independent SDK implementations and lets HTTP middleware route/validate proofs without parsing internals. Deliverable: CDDL schema file, ABNF for the HTTP header negotiation, and a reference encoder/decoder in the TS SDK.

title: Complete did:bolyra resolution algorithm per W3C DID Core §7
description: spec/did-method-bolyra.md exists but the resolution algorithm is underspecified — it doesn't define how a resolver maps did:bolyra:<commitment> to a DID Document, which verification methods to include (Ed25519VerificationKey2020 for operators, JsonWebKey2020 for providers), or how revocation status maps to the deactivated metadata property. Write the full CRUD operation definitions per W3C DID Core §7, including Read (resolve against on-chain humanTree/agentTree roots), Deactivate (revocation event), and the DID Document JSON-LD context. No Create/Update since commitments are immutable.

title: Publish machine-readable conformance test vectors (JSON)
description: No independent implementation can verify compatibility without canonical test vectors. Produce a JSON file with 30+ vectors covering: valid handshake (human+agent), expired credential rejection, stale Merkle root, scope subset violation, cumulative bit encoding violation, delegation chain (1-hop and 3-hop), nonce replay, and revoked identity. Each vector includes input witness, expected public signals, and expected pass/fail. This is the minimum bar for any IETF-track specification and directly supports the conformance runner stub in spec/.

title: Specify human tree root history buffer matching agent tree
description: IdentityRegistry.sol maintains a 30-root history buffer for agentTree but has no equivalent for humanTree — human proofs go stale the instant a new enrollment lands. The IETF draft should normatively require (RFC 2119 MUST) a root history buffer for all identity trees, with a RECOMMENDED depth of 30. Implementation: add the same rootHistory/rootIndex pattern from agentTree to humanTree in IdentityRegistry.sol, update verifyHandshake to check against the buffer, and add a test vector for 'proof generated against root N-15 still verifies'.

title: Add blinding salt to scope commitment to prevent bitmask brute-force
description: scopeCommitment = Poseidon2(bitmask, credCommitment) in AgentPolicy.circom is vulnerable to offline brute-force: the 8-bit permission space has only 256 possible values, so an observer can precompute all Poseidon2(mask, knownCredCmt) and deanonymize the scope. The fix is Poseidon3(bitmask, credCommitment, blindingSalt) where blindingSalt is a private 254-bit random input. This must be coordinated across AgentPolicy, Delegation (which chains scope commitments), and the SDK's proveHandshake. Specify the salt derivation (CSPRNG, no reuse) normatively in the IETF draft §Security Considerations.

title: Payment receipt proof circuit for agent-to-agent commerce
description: Add a PaymentReceipt circuit that binds a payment hash (tx hash or invoice ID) to a delegation chain's scopeCommitment, proving an agent was authorized to make a specific payment without revealing the delegation hierarchy. This is the missing primitive for agentic commerce: today Bolyra can prove 'this agent MAY spend up to $100' but cannot prove 'this agent DID spend $47.50 under authorization X'. The circuit takes paymentHash, amount, scopeCommitment as private inputs and outputs a receiptCommitment + amountRange (bucketed). Estimated ~25k constraints using existing Poseidon + range check patterns.

title: Stateless session token from verified handshake
description: After a successful verifyHandshake(), the SDK should emit a signed JWT-like session token (EdDSA-signed, not RSA) that encodes the verified scopeCommitment, nullifierHash, and expiry. Downstream services can verify the token without re-running the ZKP. This is the adoption unlock: without it, every microservice in an agent pipeline must independently verify Groth16 proofs or trust an opaque boolean. Add createSessionToken() and verifySessionToken() to @bolyra/sdk with a 5-minute default TTL. No new circuits needed — pure SDK work.

title: On-chain spending accumulator for financial permission tiers
description: The permission bitmask has FINANCIAL_SMALL (<$100), FINANCIAL_MEDIUM (<$10K), FINANCIAL_UNLIMITED — but nothing tracks cumulative spend. Add a spendingAccumulator mapping in IdentityRegistry keyed by (scopeCommitment, epoch) that increments on each PaymentReceipt verification. The contract reverts if cumulative epoch spend exceeds the tier ceiling. Without this, an agent with FINANCIAL_SMALL can make 1000 separate $99 transactions. The epoch (e.g., 24h rolling window) prevents the accumulator from growing unboundedly. This is table-stakes for any real commerce deployment.

title: OAuth 2.0 bridge: Bolyra proof as bearer token
description: Build a lightweight gateway (Express middleware + standalone mode) that accepts a standard OAuth 2.0 token request where the client_credentials grant includes a Bolyra proof bundle. The gateway verifies the ZKP, maps scopeCommitment to OAuth scopes, and issues a standard access_token. This lets any existing OAuth-protected API accept Bolyra-authenticated agents with zero backend changes. Target: npm package @bolyra/oauth-bridge, <100 lines of config to deploy. This is the 10-year play — meet enterprises where they are (OAuth) while pulling them toward ZKP auth.

title: Human tree root history buffer (parity with agent tree)
description: IdentityRegistry maintains a 30-root history buffer for agentTree but has no equivalent for humanTree. This means any new human enrollment instantly invalidates all in-flight human proofs. In a commerce scenario with even modest enrollment volume (10 humans/hour), proof generation (~30s with rapidsnark) will frequently race against tree updates. Add the same ROOT_HISTORY_SIZE=30 buffer pattern already proven on the agent side. This is a 2-hour change in IdentityRegistry.sol — the pattern is already implemented, just not applied to the human tree.

title: Dynamic Merkle depth with shorter default for AgentPolicy
description: AgentPolicy uses MAX_DEPTH=20 for the agent tree, but the agent registry will realistically have <1000 entries for years. Compile a second AgentPolicy artifact at depth 10 (~5k fewer constraints from BinaryMerkleRoot) and let the SDK select the artifact based on current tree depth. This cuts ~5k constraints (~10% of the circuit) and proportionally reduces proving time. The depth-20 artifact stays available as a fallback once the tree grows. Requires a new compile target in circuits/scripts/compile.js and SDK logic to pick the right .zkey.

title: Replace EdDSA verify in Delegation with Poseidon-MAC authorization
description: The Delegation circuit spends ~30k constraints on EdDSAPoseidonVerifier to prove the delegator authorized the delegation. Since the delegator's credential commitment is already bound to their pubkey via the AgentPolicy proof, the delegation can instead use a Poseidon-based HMAC: delegationToken = Poseidon(delegatorSecret, delegateeCredCommitment, delegateeScope, delegateeExpiry). This replaces ~30k EdDSA constraints with ~600 Poseidon constraints — a 40%+ reduction in total Delegation circuit size — while preserving the security property that only the delegator can produce the token.

title: Fused range-check + comparison for timestamp fields
description: AgentPolicy and Delegation both do Num2Bits(64) on expiryTimestamp and currentTimestamp, then separately run LessThan(64) which internally decomposes again. Write a RangeAndLessThan template that reuses the bit decomposition from the range check for the comparison, saving 64 constraints per timestamp pair (128 total per circuit). Small win individually, but it also eliminates a subtle bug surface: if the range check and comparator ever disagree on bit decomposition due to a template mismatch.

title: WASM witness generation with C++ rapidsnark proving pipeline
description: Currently the SDK uses snarkjs for both witness generation and proving. The witness generator compiled by circom to WASM is fast, but snarkjs Groth16 proving is 10-50x slower than rapidsnark. Wire up a hybrid pipeline in the SDK: WASM witness gen → export wtns → rapidsnark native prover. The rapidsnark binary already exists at circuits/build/rapidsnark_prover. This requires ~50 lines in sdk/src/prove.ts to shell out to rapidsnark when available and fall back to snarkjs. Benchmarks in circuits/scripts/bench_rapidsnark.js already quantify the gap — this makes it the default path.

title: ModelInstanceBinding constraint audit — deduplicate dual EdDSA verify
description: ModelInstanceBinding runs two independent EdDSAPoseidonVerifier instances (operator + provider), each ~6k constraints per the header comment but actually closer to 15k each in practice (EdDSA Poseidon verify is ~30k total, not 12k). Both verify over the same credentialCommitment message. Factor out the shared scalar multiplication setup and Poseidon message hash to save ~3-5k constraints. Also audit the actual constraint count vs the budget comment (header says ~35k but two EdDSA verifiers alone should be ~30k+). Run `circom --r1cs` and update the header with real numbers.

title: Add proof generation progress callbacks to SDK
description: Proof generation (especially full Groth16) takes 10-60+ seconds. The current SDK API is fire-and-forget — `proveHandshake()` returns a promise with no intermediate feedback. Add an optional `onProgress` callback parameter to `createHumanIdentity()`, `createAgentCredential()`, `proveHandshake()`, and `delegate()` that emits structured events like `{stage: 'witness'|'prove'|'verify', percent: number}`. This is table-stakes DX for any crypto library with long-running operations and prevents developers from assuming the SDK is frozen.

title: Typed error hierarchy with actionable messages
description: The IdentityRegistry contract defines 10+ distinct error types (StaleAgentRoot, NonceAlreadyUsed, HumanIdentityRevoked, etc.) but the SDK likely surfaces these as opaque revert strings or generic Error objects. Create a `BolyraError` base class with typed subclasses (`StaleRootError`, `ExpiredCredentialError`, `ScopeNarrowingViolation`, `NonceReplayError`) that include: the error code, a human-readable explanation, and a `suggestion` field with the remediation step (e.g., 'Re-fetch the latest Merkle root before proving'). Map every contract revert and circuit witness failure to this taxonomy.

title: Single-import hello-world handshake in <10 lines
description: The current public API requires separate calls to create identities, generate proofs, and verify — with manual Merkle tree management and nonce generation in between. Add a high-level `Bolyra.quickHandshake({humanSecret, agentCredential, provider?})` that bundles tree lookup, nonce generation, dual proof creation, and verification into one await. Keep the low-level API for production use but make the README example copy-pasteable in 8 lines. This is the single biggest adoption lever: developers who can't get a working demo in 5 minutes won't evaluate the protocol.

title: Python SDK async subprocess bridge with connection pooling
description: The Python SDK shells out to Node for every proving call. For LangChain/CrewAI agents that run many handshakes per session, the per-call subprocess overhead (Node startup + snarkjs init) dominates latency. Replace the naive subprocess bridge with a long-lived Node child process that accepts JSON-RPC over stdin/stdout, reusing the snarkjs WASM instance across calls. Expose both sync (`bolyra.prove_handshake()`) and async (`await bolyra.aprove_handshake()`) Python APIs. Target: <50ms overhead per call after first warm-up vs. current ~2s subprocess spawn.

title: Fluent delegation chain builder API
description: The Delegation circuit enforces scope narrowing, cumulative bit encoding, and expiry constraints — but developers must manually compute scope bitmasks, track previousScopeCommitment across hops, and serialize each proof. Add a `DelegationChain.from(agentCredential).narrow({permissions: ['READ_DATA', 'FINANCIAL_SMALL'], expiry: '2h'}).to(delegateeCredential).build()` builder that: (1) resolves permission names to bitmask bits, (2) validates cumulative encoding client-side before proving, (3) chains scopeCommitments automatically across hops, and (4) returns a serialized `DelegationToken` ready for on-chain submission. Replace raw bitmask math with named permissions throughout.

title: Framework-agnostic handshake middleware (Express/FastAPI)
description: Create a thin server-side middleware that verifies Bolyra handshake proofs on incoming agent HTTP requests. LangChain RemoteRunnable, CrewAI task endpoints, and AutoGen agent-to-agent calls all use HTTP under the hood. A single `bolyraVerifyMiddleware(req, res, next)` (Express) and `@bolyra_verify` decorator (FastAPI) that checks the `X-Bolyra-Proof` header against IdentityRegistry would let any framework plug in ZK auth with one line of code — no framework-specific tool class needed. Ship as `@bolyra/middleware` and `bolyra[middleware]` extras.

title: Map multi-agent task delegation to Bolyra delegation circuit
description: In CrewAI and AutoGen, an orchestrator agent delegates sub-tasks to worker agents. Today this is trust-on-config — any agent can claim any capability. Add a `BolyraTaskRouter` that, before dispatching a task, generates a Delegation proof narrowing the orchestrator's permissions to the subset the worker needs (e.g., READ_DATA + FINANCIAL_SMALL for a price-lookup agent). The worker must present this delegated proof to the target service. This makes the 8-bit permission bitmask and scope-narrowing circuit directly useful in real multi-agent workflows.

title: Proof caching with TTL for high-throughput agent loops
description: LangChain agent loops and CrewAI crews can make 50-200 tool calls per task. Generating a fresh Groth16 proof per call (~2s with snarkjs, ~200ms with rapidsnark) is prohibitive. Add a `ProofCache` to the SDK that caches a valid (humanProof, agentProof) pair keyed by sessionNonce, reusing it within the nonce's TTL window. The cache invalidates on scope change, expiry, or nonce rotation. This is the single biggest friction point for framework adoption — without it, ZK auth adds unacceptable latency to agentic workflows.

title: Machine-parseable error codes for agent retry logic
description: The IdentityRegistry contract uses custom errors (StaleAgentRoot, NonceAlreadyUsed, etc.) but the SDK and integrations surface these as opaque revert strings. LangChain and AutoGen both have built-in retry/fallback patterns that need structured error types to decide whether to retry (stale root → re-fetch root and retry), re-authenticate (expired credential), or fail hard (revoked identity). Add an enum `BolyraErrorCode` with categories RETRYABLE, REAUTH, FATAL, and map every contract error + SDK failure to it. Ship in both TS and Python SDKs.

title: OpenAI-compatible function/tool schema for Bolyra auth actions
description: AutoGen and LangChain both support OpenAI-style function calling schemas. Publish a canonical JSON Schema for Bolyra auth actions (authenticate, delegate, verify_peer, check_permissions) that any LLM can invoke as a tool call. This lets agents running on GPT-4, Claude, or open models authenticate via Bolyra without framework-specific BaseTool subclasses — they just need the schema and an HTTP endpoint. Pair with the middleware proposal for a complete 'drop-in ZK auth for any agent framework' story.

title: Pin expected scope in IdentityRegistry to prevent revocation bypass
description: HumanUniqueness derives nullifierHash = Poseidon2(scope, secret). Revocation works by flagging a nullifierHash in revokedIdentities. But scope is a public input chosen by the prover — if the contract doesn't enforce scope == HANDSHAKE_SCOPE_CONSTANT, a revoked human can supply an arbitrary scope value, produce a different nullifierHash, and bypass the revocation check entirely. Fix: add an immutable HANDSHAKE_SCOPE constant to IdentityRegistry and require pubSignals[scope_index] == HANDSHAKE_SCOPE in verifyHandshake(). This is a ~20-line contract change plus test vectors.

title: Add root history buffer for humanTree (parity with agentTree)
description: IdentityRegistry maintains a 30-root history buffer for agentTree so in-flight proofs survive concurrent enrollments, but humanTree has no such buffer. A single new human enrollment changes the LeanIMT root and immediately invalidates every in-flight HumanUniqueness proof. In production with concurrent enrollments this causes persistent proof failures. Fix: add a parallel uint256[30] humanTreeRootHistory ring buffer and isKnownHumanRoot() check, matching the existing agentTree pattern. Contract change only — circuits are unaffected.

title: Verify delegator enrollment in Delegation circuit
description: The Delegation circuit takes delegatorCredCommitment as a private input and verifies it matches previousScopeCommitment via Poseidon(delegatorScope, delegatorCredCommitment). However, the delegator's tree membership is never proven — previousScopeCommitment chain-links are trusted transitively from an initial AgentPolicy proof, but if the contract ever allows delegation without a prior handshake (e.g., future API expansion), a fabricated delegatorCredCommitment that hashes to the right scopeCommitment would pass. Defense-in-depth: add a delegator Merkle inclusion proof (same pattern as the existing delegatee CIP-1 fix) so the circuit independently proves both parties are enrolled. Adds ~10k constraints but closes the transitive-trust assumption.

title: Add blinding salt to scopeCommitment to prevent bitmask extraction
description: scopeCommitment = Poseidon2(permissionBitmask, credentialCommitment) is a public output of AgentPolicy and each Delegation hop. The permission bitmask is only 8 meaningful bits (256 values) and credentialCommitment is public on-chain (it's a Merkle leaf). An observer can brute-force all 256 possible bitmask values against the known credentialCommitment to recover the agent's exact permissions, destroying the privacy model that keeps scope bits private. Fix: add a random blindingSalt private input and compute scopeCommitment = Poseidon3(permissionBitmask, credentialCommitment, blindingSalt). Requires circuit recompile + zkey regen + SDK update to generate/pass the salt.

title: Design revocation accumulator for off-chain proof verification
description: All revocation state (revokedIdentities, usedNonces, usedDelegationNullifiers) lives exclusively in IdentityRegistry contract storage. Any off-chain verifier (SDK-level verifyHandshake, MCP proxy, gateway) that checks only the SNARK math will accept proofs from revoked identities or replayed nonces. Before shipping off-chain verification or the batched-checkpoint model, implement a revocation accumulator: either a sparse Merkle tree of revoked nullifiers committed on-chain (verifiable via storage proof) or an append-only revocation list with signed checkpoints. Without this, off-chain verification is unsound. Spec the accumulator structure and add a circuit-level non-membership proof.
